questions.stem: A 32-year-old man presents with a 3-month history of gradually worsening exertional dyspnoea and ankle swelling. He has no chest pain, palpitations, or syncope. He drinks 2–3 units of alcohol per week. ECG shows sinus rhythm, left axis deviation, and incomplete LBBB. CXR shows cardiomegaly. Echocardiogram shows a dilated left ventricle with global hypokinesia, LVEF 35%, and no valvular abnormalities. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Global hypokinesia with LV dilation and no other identifiable cause (no chest pain, minimal alcohol, no hypertrophy) supports idiopathic dilated cardiomyopathy. Ischaemic cardiomyopathy would typically have regional wall motion abnormalities and coronary risk factors.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old woman presents with acute severe breathlessness and frothy pink sputum. BP 95/60, HR 110, O2 sat 88% on air. Bibasal crackles and S3 gallop. CXR shows pulmonary oedema and cardiomegaly. ECG shows sinus tachycardia and poor R-wave progression. Troponin mildly elevated (40 ng/L). She has no chest pain. Past medical history includes type 2 diabetes and hypertension. Echocardiogram shows a dilated LV with LVEF 25%. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The echo findings of a chronically dilated LV with severe systolic dysfunction (not regional wall motion) indicate chronic dilated cardiomyopathy now acutely decompensated. The absence of chest pain and typical ECG changes make MI unlikely, and the lack of a viral prodrome argues against myocarditis.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman presents with sudden onset left-sided weakness and dysarthria. She has no history of AF or stroke. She takes an oral contraceptive. MRI shows a right MCA infarction. ECG is sinus rhythm. Echocardiogram reveals a dilated LV with LVEF 30% and a mobile thrombus at the apex. What is the single most likely underlying diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The presence of LV thrombus in a dilated, poorly functioning ventricle is characteristic of dilated cardiomyopathy. Ischaemic cardiomyopathy could also cause LV thrombus but is less likely without prior MI or chest pain; the other options do not typically cause apical LV thrombus.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman presents at 3 weeks postpartum with progressive dyspnoea and orthopnoea. She had a normal pregnancy and delivery. BP 110/70, HR 100. JVP elevated, bilateral pedal oedema. Echo shows dilated LV, LVEF 35%. No prior heart disease. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The presentation within 5 months postpartum with LV systolic dysfunction in the absence of hypertension, acute onset, or thyroid abnormalities is classic for peripartum cardiomyopathy. Pre-eclampsia would involve hypertension, and PE would have acute respiratory symptoms.

questions.stem: A 26-year-old man has a brother who died suddenly at age 32. Screening echocardiogram shows LV dilation and LVEF 35%. He is asymptomatic. ECG shows LBBB. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The family history of sudden death and LV dilation with systolic impairment in a young individual strongly suggests familial dilated cardiomyopathy. HCM would show hypertrophy, ARVC affects the RV, and myocarditis is typically acute.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man presents with progressive exertional dyspnoea. Examination reveals a displaced apex beat and a third heart sound. Echocardiography shows left ventricular end-diastolic diameter 6.5 cm and left ventricular ejection fraction 30%. What is the most appropriate next investigation to determine the aetiology of his cardiomyopathy?
questions.explanation: In new-onset dilated cardiomyopathy, coronary angiography is recommended to exclude ischaemic heart disease, a common reversible cause. Cardiac MRI is useful but typically performed after coronary assessment if no ischaemia is found.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman presents with acute onset of chest pain and dyspnoea a week after a flu-like illness. Her ECG shows ST-segment elevation in the precordial leads. Echocardiography reveals severe left ventricular dilatation with an ejection fraction of 25%. High-sensitivity troponin is elevated. Which investigation would best confirm the diagnosis of acute myocarditis as the cause?
options: Cardiac MRI with late gadolinium enhancement
questions.explanation: Cardiac MRI with LGE is the non-invasive gold standard for diagnosing acute myocarditis, showing characteristic subepicardial or mid-wall enhancement. Endomyocardial biopsy is the definitive test but is invasive and reserved for refractory cases.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with known dilated cardiomyopathy (LVEF 35%) presents with palpitations. ECG shows atrial fibrillation with a ventricular rate of 120 bpm. He has been stable on bisoprolol and ramipril. What is the single most appropriate investigation before considering electrical cardioversion?
questions.explanation: TOE is the most sensitive test to exclude left atrial thrombus prior to cardioversion, which is especially important in dilated cardiomyopathy where atrial enlargement increases thrombus risk.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman, 3 weeks postpartum, presents with orthopnoea and peripheral oedema. Echocardiography shows a left ventricular ejection fraction of 40% and mild LV dilatation. She has no prior cardiac history. Which diagnostic finding is most consistent with peripartum cardiomyopathy?
questions.explanation: Peripartum cardiomyopathy is defined by LVEF <45% and LV dilatation occurring in the peripartum period without another cause. The combination of reduced EF and dilatation is most specific.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with dyspnoea has a suboptimal echocardiogram due to poor acoustic windows. Suspicion of dilated cardiomyopathy remains. Which imaging modality is considered the gold standard for assessing left ventricular volumes and ejection fraction in this setting?
questions.explanation: Cardiac MRI is the gold standard for accurate and reproducible LV volumes and EF, especially when echocardiography is inadequate. It also provides tissue characterisation to aid aetiological diagnosis.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man presents with progressive exertional dyspnoea and fatigue over 6 months. Echocardiogram shows a dilated left ventricle with an ejection fraction (EF) of 35%. He is normotensive (BP 118/76 mmHg), heart rate 72 bpm, in sinus rhythm, and has no signs of fluid overload. Which drug is most appropriate to initiate as first-line disease-modifying therapy?
questions.explanation: NICE guidelines recommend an ACE inhibitor as first-line therapy for patients with heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF) to reduce morbidity and mortality. Beta-blockers are added after stabilisation on an ACE inhibitor. Spironolactone is second-line for those with persistent symptoms. Furosemide is for congestion, and digoxin is reserved for symptomatic HF or rate control in AF.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman with known dilated cardiomyopathy (EF 40%) is taking ramipril 5 mg twice daily and bisoprolol 5 mg once daily. She remains breathless on mild exertion (NYHA III). Her blood pressure is 110/70 mmHg, heart rate 68 bpm, and she has no significant peripheral oedema. Serum potassium is 4.5 mmol/L and eGFR >60 mL/min. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Increase ramipril to 10 mg twice daily
Increase bisoprolol to 10 mg once daily
questions.explanation: In patients with HFrEF who remain symptomatic (NYHA II-IV) despite optimal ACE inhibitor and beta-blocker therapy, NICE recommends adding a mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist (e.g., spironolactone) to improve outcomes. Ivabradine is reserved for those with sinus rhythm ≥75 bpm despite beta-blocker or if beta-blocker intolerant. Digoxin is for symptomatic HF or AF. Further up-titration of ACEi or beta-blocker is possible but adding spironolactone is the guideline-directed step.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man with known dilated cardiomyopathy presents with acute severe dyspnoea and frothy pink sputum. On examination, he is clammy, BP 85/50 mmHg, heart rate 120 bpm, JVP elevated, and widespread crackles. ECG shows sinus tachycardia. Chest X-ray confirms pulmonary oedema. Which drug is CONTRAINDICATED in this acute setting?
questions.explanation: Intravenous beta-blockers (e.g., bisoprolol) are contraindicated in acute decompensated heart failure with pulmonary oedema or cardiogenic shock because of their negative inotropic effect, which can exacerbate hypotension and reduce cardiac output. Furosemide reduces preload, dobutamine is an inotrope for cardiogenic shock, morphine relieves anxiety and reduces preload, and nitrates are vasodilators—all appropriate.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old man with dilated cardiomyopathy (EF 30%) develops palpitations and breathlessness. ECG shows atrial fibrillation with a ventricular rate of 150 bpm. He is haemodynamically stable (BP 105/70 mmHg). He is already on ramipril, bisoprolol, and spironolactone. Which drug is CONTRAINDICATED for rate or rhythm control in this patient?
questions.explanation: Verapamil is a non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker with significant negative inotropic effects and is contraindicated in heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF). It can worsen cardiac function. Digoxin, beta-blockers (metoprolol), and amiodarone are safe and commonly used. Warfarin is indicated for anticoagulation in AF with HF, not contraindicated.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman who gave birth 2 months ago presents with dyspnoea and orthopnoea. Echocardiogram shows a dilated left ventricle with EF 35%, consistent with peripartum cardiomyopathy. She is currently breastfeeding her infant. She is haemodynamically stable, in sinus rhythm, with no significant fluid overload. Which drug would be CONTRAINDICATED or should be avoided in this patient?
questions.explanation: Spironolactone is not recommended during breastfeeding due to its potential anti-androgenic effects in the infant (e.g., gynecomastia, hormonal changes). Enalapril and other ACE inhibitors are considered safe in breastfeeding. Beta-blockers (bisoprolol) are safe, furosemide can be used with caution, and digoxin is also safe. Standard heart failure therapy should be instituted but spironolactone should be avoided.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with no prior cardiac history presents to his GP with progressive exertional dyspnoea over 3 months. He has no ankle oedema. Examination: BP 130/80, HR 90, JVP not elevated, chest clear. ECG shows sinus tachycardia with left bundle branch block. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
questions.explanation: In a patient with suspected heart failure, the first investigation to confirm the diagnosis and assess LVEF is an echocardiogram (NICE CG187). Starting therapy without a confirmed diagnosis is premature; other tests are not first-line.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old woman with known dilated cardiomyopathy (LVEF 30% on recent echo) is on optimal medical therapy with ramipril, bisoprolol, and spironolactone. She is in NYHA class II, BP 110/70, HR 65, and has no oedema. What is the most appropriate next step?
questions.explanation: NICE recommends ICD for primary prevention of sudden cardiac death in patients with LVEF ≤35% and NYHA II–III despite ≥3 months of optimal medical therapy, provided life expectancy >1 year (TA314). The patient meets criteria; other options are not indicated now.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man with known dilated cardiomyopathy presents to the ED with severe breathlessness, unable to speak in full sentences, O2 sat 85% on room air, BP 95/60, HR 110, JVP elevated, and diffuse crackles on auscultation. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
questions.explanation: In acute pulmonary oedema with severe respiratory distress, immediate non-invasive ventilation (CPAP or BiPAP) improves oxygenation and reduces work of breathing (NICE NG19). Diuretics and inotropes are given subsequently; beta-blockers are contraindicated acutely.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old woman with dilated cardiomyopathy (LVEF 25%) on furosemide, ramipril, and carvedilol has a routine ECG showing QTc 520 ms. She is asymptomatic and has normal serum potassium (4.0 mmol/L). What is the most appropriate next step?
questions.explanation: Hypomagnesaemia is a common, reversible cause of QTc prolongation in heart failure patients on loop diuretics, and should be checked before considering device therapy or drug changes (NICE CG187). Sotalol prolongs QT further; ICD is not first-line without reversible causes.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman diagnosed with peripartum cardiomyopathy (LVEF 35% 2 months postpartum) is now asymptomatic on ramipril and bisoprolol. BP 110/70, HR 75, JVP not elevated. She asks about future pregnancies. What is the most appropriate next step?
questions.explanation: Peripartum cardiomyopathy has a high risk of recurrence and mortality in subsequent pregnancies. Current guidelines strongly advise against further pregnancies and emphasise effective contraception. Monitoring with echocardiography is important, but the immediate next step is counselling.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man presents with a 2-month history of progressive exertional dyspnoea and ankle swelling. ECG shows sinus rhythm with left bundle branch block. Echocardiography reveals a dilated left ventricle (LVEDD 6.2 cm) and an ejection fraction of 35%. He has no previous medical history. Which single finding on further assessment most urgently requires referral for consideration of an implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD)?
options: Family history of ischaemic heart disease
questions.explanation: Non-sustained VT in a patient with dilated cardiomyopathy and LVEF ≤35% is a strong predictor of sudden cardiac death and triggers ICD consideration per NICE guidelines. NYHA class II, moderate MR, and QRS duration alone do not meet threshold; family history of IHD is not specific.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with known dilated cardiomyopathy (LVEF 25%) presents to the emergency department with acute severe breathlessness. On examination, he is pale, BP 85/50 mmHg, pulse 110 bpm, JVP elevated, and has bibasal crackles. Which clinical finding confirms that he has progressed to cardiogenic shock?
questions.explanation: Cardiogenic shock is defined by sustained hypotension with signs of hypoperfusion. Cool, clammy peripheries indicate poor tissue perfusion. Crackles and raised JVP are signs of congestion but not shock; tachycardia is non-specific; peripheral oedema is chronic.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old woman with dilated cardiomyopathy (LVEF 30%) in sinus rhythm presents with sudden-onset right-sided weakness and slurred speech. CT head confirms acute ischaemic stroke. Which echocardiographic finding is most likely to be the cause?
questions.explanation: In dilated cardiomyopathy, even without atrial fibrillation, left ventricular apical thrombus is a common source of systemic embolism due to blood stasis. Atrial septal defect is not associated; vegetation suggests infective endocarditis; aortic calcification is not embolic; left atrial enlargement alone is not causative.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman, 3 weeks postpartum, presents with progressive breathlessness and orthopnoea. Echocardiography shows a dilated left ventricle with an ejection fraction of 30%. Which feature is most suggestive of peripartum cardiomyopathy rather than another cause of dilated cardiomyopathy?
options: Left bundle branch block on ECG
Onset of symptoms within the last month of pregnancy or first 5 months postpartum
questions.explanation: Peripartum cardiomyopathy is defined by onset of heart failure in the last month of pregnancy or within 5 months postpartum. LV thrombus can occur in any DCM; LBBB is non-specific; family history is not diagnostic; normal coronaries are seen in many DCMs.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man with dilated cardiomyopathy has been on optimal medical therapy (ACE inhibitor, beta-blocker, spironolactone) for 6 months. He is NYHA class II. Which echocardiographic finding would indicate that he meets the criteria for primary prevention with an implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD)?
questions.explanation: NICE guidance recommends ICD for primary prevention in non-ischaemic DCM if LVEF ≤35% and NYHA II or III despite optimal therapy. LV dimensions or other echo parameters alone do not meet the threshold; MR and RV pressure are not criteria.
